ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fermented tea leaf salad (lahpet thoke). Mapped to the nearest parent class 'salad food product' (FOODON_03301710) as a closest_parent match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Salads) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Burmese fermented tea leaf salad in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fermented tea leaf salad. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Tea Leaf Salad (No Meat) is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small plate as typically served at a Singaporean hawker stall or Burmese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Tea Leave Salad (No Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tea Leave Salad (No Meat), cr_id G00010611. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.0 g/100g based on the known fatty acid profile of the primary fat contributors (frying oil and peanuts) in this dish, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on fermented/pickled vegetable salad with mixed ingredients
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese fermented tea leaf salad (lahpet thoke). GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable salad entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables. The dish is predominantly fat (44.5 g/100g) and very low in carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g), which strongly suppresses postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 5.9 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.8 (very low). This estimate is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; primary contributors are fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 50 g and fish sauce and dried shrimp should be reduced or omitted to bring sodium below 400 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity and promote weight gain unfavourable for T2D management
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish to small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced oil preparation; the absence of dietary fibre and high saturated fat load (~7 g/100g) worsen insulin resistance and should be offset by pairing with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.5 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7 g/100g), cholesterol (26 mg/100g), and sodium (810 mg/100g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food only; the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and high caloric density presents a compounded cardiovascular risk that is not offset by the modest polyphenol content of fermented tea leaves.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~7 g/100g exceeds the 5 g gate; chilli is a nightshade ingredient with potential to trigger joint flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard preparation due to the pro-inflammatory combination of high sodium, high saturated fat, and nightshade ingredient (chilli); the polyphenol benefit of fermented tea leaves is insufficient to offset these risks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea Leaf Salad (No Meat), known in Burmese as Lahpet Thoke or Laphet Thoke, is a traditional Burmese fermented tea leaf salad that has become a popular dish in Singapore's hawker centres and Burmese restaurants. The dish is built around laphet — fermented and pickled tea leaves that are the defining ingredient of Burmese cuisine — combined with fried egg, fried garlic, fried peanuts, fried sesame seeds, dried shrimp, fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, tomato, and shredded cabbage. The no-meat variant omits chicken or other meats but retains dried shrimp and fish sauce, making it non-vegetarian and non-halal. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, tangy, slightly bitter from the tea leaves, and nutty from the peanuts and sesame. The texture is complex: crunchy from fried garlic and peanuts, soft from the fermented leaves, and crisp from fresh vegetables. Nutritionally, this dish is extremely high in fat (44.5 g/100g) and calories (430 kcal/100g), with very low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g/100g). Sodium is critically high at 810 mg/100g, driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7 g/100g. The glycemic index is low (estimated ~32) due to the minimal carbohydrate content, but this does not make the dish suitable for metabolic conditions given its fat and sodium burden. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease in standard preparation. Fermented tea leaves contain polyphenols with antioxidant properties, but these benefits are negated by the high-sodium, high-fat preparation. In Singapore, this dish is commonly consumed at breakfast or teatime and is associated with Burmese cultural occasions.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010611), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is adapted from the known fatty acid profile of the dish's primary fat contributors and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / 2021 International GI Tables, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish. This is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, likely reflecting the low-fibre nature of the fermented tea leaf and fried ingredient preparation. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. 2. saturated_fat_g (7.0 g/100g) is an adapted estimate: HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; the value is derived from the known fatty acid composition of the primary fat contributors (frying oil ~60% of fat, peanuts ~15% of fat) consistent with USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for these ingredients. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel for this specific HPB dish preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~32, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database mixed vegetable salad entries and the 2021 International GI Tables; no direct GI measurement exists for lahpet thoke. 5. The dish is classified under 'Mixed' food group rather than 'Beverages' as the input record incorrectly assigned it to the Beverages group; tea leaf salad is a solid food dish, not a beverage. 6. is_halal set to false: dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce which may not meet halal certification standards in all contexts; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
